content:
# 2 John Chapter 1 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.1 — "The elder" vs. "This letter is from John, the elder"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The elder to [the] elect lady" | ho presbyteros eklektē kyria |
| LOGOS | "The elder to the elect lady" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "This letter is from John, the elder" | Adds "John"; adds "this letter is from" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author identifies himself only as "the elder" (ho presbyteros) — not by name. PNEUMA adds "John" to the text, identifying the author by a name not present in the letter. While tradition attributes the letter to John, the text itself uses only the title. Translation should render what the text says, not what tradition infers.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds authorial identification not in the text.

---

### v.5 — "dear lady" vs. "dear friends"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "lady" | kyria |
| LOGOS | "dear lady" | Same with English courtesy |
| PNEUMA | "dear friends" | Plural "friends" for singular kyria |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says kyria ("lady") — singular, addressing the "elect lady" of v.1. PNEUMA changes to plural "friends," apparently because the metaphor (a church) implies a group. But the author maintains the literary fiction of addressing a woman throughout the letter. Switching to "friends" breaks the metaphor.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA breaks the literary fiction.

---

### v.7 — "those who do not confess the coming of Jesus Christ in the flesh" vs. "they deny that Jesus Christ came in a real body"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "those not confessing Yeshua Christos coming in [the] flesh" | hoi mē homologountes Iēsoun Christon erchomenon en sarki |
| LOGOS | "those who do not confess the coming of Jesus Christ in the flesh" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "They deny that Jesus Christ came in a real body" | "Deny" for "not confess"; "real body" for "flesh"; "came" for present participle |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Three issues: (1) "Not confess" (mē homologountes) is not the same as "deny" — failing to confess is passive, denying is active. (2) "Flesh" (sarki) is the theologically loaded term — "real body" loses the sarx/pneuma (flesh/spirit) opposition central to Johannine theology. (3) The participle is present (erchomenon, "coming"), not aorist ("came") — the ongoing reality of the incarnation, not just the past event.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. "Coming" (present) vs. "came" (past) affects whether the incarnation is a past event or an ongoing reality.

---

### v.12 — "talk face to face" vs. "mouth to mouth"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "speak mouth to mouth" | stoma pros stoma |
| LOGOS | "talk face to face" | English idiom for same meaning |
| PNEUMA | "talk with you face to face" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. While EMET's "mouth to mouth" is strictly literal, it creates a misleading image in English (suggesting a kiss). LOGOS's "face to face" is the natural English equivalent of direct, personal conversation. All three agree on meaning; this is a question of idiom. EMET's literalism is noted but not adopted.

**Status:** Minor. All three agree on meaning; idiom difference only.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adds to text | 1 | "John" added to authorial identification (v.1) |
| PNEUMA breaks literary fiction | 1 | "Friends" for "lady" (v.5) |
| PNEUMA changes tense/voice | 1 | "Deny/came/real body" for "not confess/coming/flesh" (v.7) |

**Overall pattern:** 2 John's most significant disagreement is v.7 — the present participle "coming" (erchomenon) vs. past tense "came." The Johannine author uses the present to insist that the incarnation is not a completed past event but an ongoing reality: Christ is coming in the flesh — his embodiment continues. PNEUMA's past tense treats it as a historical fact to be acknowledged, losing the ongoing theological claim.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
